title: STANDARD FOR CANNED BABY FOODS CXS 73-1981

sections.section: STANDARD FOR CANNED BABY FOODS CXS 73-1981
sections.summary: Document title: Standard for Canned Baby Foods (CXS 73-1981).
Adoption and amendment history: standard adopted in 1981 and amended in 1983, 1985, 1987, 1989, 2017 and 2023.

2023 amendments moved food additive provisions into the General Standard for Food Additives (GSFA) and updated provisions in Section 7 (Packaging) and Section 9.5 (Information for utilization) following the 46th CAC decisions.

Scope: defines baby foods as products intended primarily for the weaning period and adaptation to ordinary food (ready-to-eat or dry requiring reconstitution), excludes infant formula and processed cereal-based foods for infants/young children, and requires ready-to-eat foods to be heat processed and dry forms processed by physical means to prevent spoilage.
Definitions: 'infant' means a person not more than 12 months old; 'young children' means persons older than 12 months up to 3 years; 'calorie' means kilocalorie (1 kJ = 0.239 kcal).

Essential composition and quality factors: baby foods may be made from any suitable nutritive material (including spices), vitamins/minerals may only be added per the legislation of the country (from advisory lists) and total sodium in ready-to-eat products must not exceed 200 mg Na/100 g with no added salt permitted in fruit-based products; ready-to-eat products must meet defined consistency categories (strained or junior) and ingredients must be clean, of good quality with excessive fibre removed and fish/meat practically free of bone pieces.

Composition specifics: baby foods may be prepared from any suitable nutritive material including spices; vitamins and minerals may only be added in accordance with national legislation and should be selected from the Advisory Lists, sodium from added nutrients must be within the sodium limits, and total sodium in ready-to-eat products shall not exceed 200 mg Na/100 g with no added salt permitted in fruit-based products.

Food additives: acidity regulators, antioxidants, emulsifiers, packaging gases and thickeners listed for food category 13.2 in the GSFA are acceptable; flavourings have specified maximum use levels (vanillin and ethyl vanillin 70 mg/kg, vanilla extract GMP); and carry-over of additives is only permitted if levels in raw materials do not exceed maxima and the final food does not contain more of the additive than would be introduced under good manufacturing practice.

Contaminants: products must comply with maximum levels in the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995) and with Codex maximum residue limits for pesticides and veterinary drugs.

Hygiene: products should be prepared and handled per the General Principles of Food Hygiene and relevant Codex codes, and must comply with any microbiological criteria established under the relevant Codex guidelines.
Packaging: the product shall be packed in containers that safeguard hygienic and other qualities, and ready-to-eat products must be packed in hermetically sealed containers.

Fill of container: ready-to-eat products must fill at least 80% v/v for containers <150 g, 85% v/v for 150–250 g, and 90% v/v for >250 g of the container's water capacity (volume of distilled water at 20°C the sealed container holds when full).

Labelling (specific provisions): in addition to the General Standard for Labelling, labels must state the product name reflecting the major/characterizing ingredient and intended consistency/use, a full ingredients list with vitamins/minerals grouped separately, specified nutrition declaration requirements, date marking and storage instructions, preparation/use directions, and products must not be presented as breast‑milk substitutes.
Name of the food: the product name must be that of the major or characterizing ingredient(s) accompanied by words indicating consistency or intended use.

List of ingredients: a complete ingredients list must be declared in descending order of proportion, except added vitamins and minerals which must be grouped separately (and need not be in descending order within those groups), and specific names for ingredients and additives must be declared with class names optionally included.

Declaration of nutritive value: labels must state energy (kcal and/or kJ) and grams of protein, carbohydrate and fat per 100 g and per suggested serving, and must declare the total quantity of each vitamin and mineral added per 100 g and per suggested serving.

Date marking and storage: the date of minimum durability ('best before') shall be given as day/month/year (or month/year for shelf‑life >3 months), with permitted alternatives (e.g., 'end (year)'), and any special storage conditions affecting validity must be indicated and placed near the date marking where practicable.
Requires the label or accompanying leaflet to state directions for preparation, use, and storage/keeping of the food both before and after the container has been opened.
States that the products covered by this standard are not breast‑milk substitutes and must not be presented as such.
Requires that compliance checks use the methods of analysis and sampling contained in the Recommended Methods of Analysis and Sampling (CXS 234-1999) relevant to this standard's provisions.
